mnemonics.title: STAKEHOLDER Analysis Steps
mnemonics.content:
S - Study project environment
T - Think about all affected parties
A - Ask: Who benefits, funds, uses, regulates?
K - Keep track in Stakeholder Register
E - Evaluate power and interest
H - Hierarchy classification (Grid)
O - Organize communication strategy
L - List roles and responsibilities
D - Define engagement approach
E - Establish monitoring process
R - Review and update regularly

acronyms.usage: Used to define clear roles and responsibilities for requirements activities and deliverables, ensuring no gaps in accountability during the groundwork phase.
Criteria for defining project objectives and success metrics during scope definition and requirements management planning.

acronyms.expansion: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have
acronyms.usage: Technique for requirements prioritization during the groundwork phase to establish scope boundaries and manage stakeholder expectations.
An iterative software development methodology that emphasizes early groundwork in the form of business modeling and requirements elucidation.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 2x2 matrix: Top-Right (High Power/High Interest = Manage Closely), Top-Left (High Power/Low Interest = Keep Satisfied), Bottom-Right (Low Power/High Interest = Keep Informed), Bottom-Left (Low Power/Low Interest = Monitor). Think of it as a compass: satisfy the powerful, inform the concerned.

Visualize a spreadsheet matrix where rows represent requirements and columns represent project artifacts (design, code, test cases). Each cell contains a link showing which artifact satisfies which requirement. Like a web connecting upstream requirements to downstream deliverables.

Think of an equilateral triangle with 'Scope' at the top and 'Time' and 'Cost' at the base. The ground represents quality. Any change to one side requires adjustment to others to maintain the triangle's integrity - this is the fundamental tradeoff project managers navigate.

analogies.analogy: Stakeholder identification is like mapping an ecosystem before exploring a new territory. You must understand who lives there (stakeholders), their relationships, and how they might react to your presence before planning activities.

A requirements baseline is like a legal contract - it represents the agreed-upon scope of work. Just as contract changes require formal negotiation, requirement changes require formal change control procedures.
Scope creep is like a construction project where the owner keeps adding rooms without adjusting timelines or budget. Without clear boundaries and change control, the project becomes unsustainable.
The Requirements Management Plan is like the constitution for requirements activities - it establishes the fundamental rules, processes, and structures that govern all subsequent requirements work.
